reasoning: The `set` external_body ensures only constrain `self.seq@` (the Ghost field). The concrete `ar: [A;N]` is never mentioned in any ensures, so there is no formal guarantee the runtime array is updated. This is a real ghost-runtime desync gap inherent to the Array abstraction.

This PHI merely shows an empty array vacuously satisfies `wf()` — that's standard behavior. The fact that `init2zero` is only for `usize` is a design choice, not a spec gap. The property proven is trivially true and reveals no issue.

This is just `Seq::update` semantics — updating index `i` with `v2` yields `v2` at index `i`. That two writes to the same index lose the first value is standard array behavior, not a spec gap. No system requires write-history tracking for a simple array.

Two arrays with `arr1.seq@ =~= arr2.seq@` have extensionally equal ghost sequences, so element-wise equality follows from `Seq` axioms. The concrete `ar` fields differing is the same ghost-runtime desync already captured by PHI 1 — this PHI itself just proves a tautology about extensional equality.

Rust arrays can store any value of type `A` — for `usize`, all values including `usize::MAX` are valid. There is no domain restriction needed. This is standard `Seq::update` semantics, not a spec gap.
